narration.content: In the ancient city of {{place:jerusalem:Jerusalem}}, in a time when kings still sat on thrones and prophets still walked the dusty streets, there lived a man named {{person:lehi:Lehi}}. He had a good family and a comfortable home, and he had raised his sons to work hard and to know the God of their fathers. But the world outside his door was troubled. The people of Jerusalem had turned away from God, and the city that had once been holy was now full of wickedness.

That year, many prophets came to warn the people: repent, or Jerusalem will be destroyed. Most people laughed at them. Some threw stones. But Lehi did not laugh. He listened, and his heart ached for his people. So he did what he had always done when the weight of the world pressed heavy on his shoulders — he knelt down and prayed. Not a quick, quiet prayer, either. Lehi poured out his whole heart to God, pleading for the people he loved.

narration.alt: Lehi kneeling in prayer as a pillar of fire appears on a rock before him
prompts: An ancient Hebrew man in robes kneeling on rocky ground before a towering pillar of brilliant white-gold fire that rests on a large stone, his hands raised in awe, the fire illuminating his face and casting long dramatic shadows across the barren landscape outside Jerusalem, warm golden and amber light contrasting with deep blue twilight sky, epic biblical illustration style, painterly realism, warm dramatic lighting, rich color palette, sense of awe and grandeur, Arnold Friberg meets contemporary concept art --ar 16:9 --v 6.1 --style raw

A pillar of radiant fire burning upon a desert rock at dusk, seen from behind a kneeling man in ancient Hebrew robes, the fire so bright it washes the entire scene in warm gold, distant silhouette of Jerusalem's walls on the horizon, the man trembling with his face toward the light, epic biblical illustration style, painterly realism, warm dramatic lighting, rich color palette, sense of awe and grandeur --ar 16:9 --v 6.1 --style raw

narration.content: And then something happened that changed Lehi's life forever. As he prayed, a pillar of fire appeared and came to rest on a rock right in front of him. The light was overwhelming. Lehi saw things and heard things that shook him to his bones — he trembled so hard he could barely stand. When it was over, he stumbled home and collapsed onto his bed, completely overcome by what he had experienced.

But the vision was not finished with him. As he lay there, the heavens themselves seemed to open above him. Lehi saw {{person:god-the-father:God}} sitting on His throne, surrounded by countless angels singing praises that echoed through eternity. Then he saw someone descending from heaven — a being whose brightness outshone the sun at noon. Twelve others followed, their radiance brighter than the stars. They came down to the earth, and the first one stood before Lehi and handed him a book.

narration.alt: Lehi's vision of heaven opening with God on His throne surrounded by angels
prompts: A breathtaking vision of heaven opening above a man lying on a bed in a simple ancient room, golden light pouring down through parted clouds revealing a magnificent throne surrounded by thousands of luminous angels in white robes singing, the light filling the room with warmth, a radiant figure descending from the heavens with twelve others trailing behind like stars falling in formation, epic biblical illustration style, painterly realism, warm dramatic lighting, rich color palette, overwhelming sense of divine majesty --ar 16:9 --v 6.1 --style raw

A luminous being brighter than the noonday sun descending from an open heaven, twelve radiant figures following behind, golden light cascading downward through layers of clouds and angelic hosts, viewed from below as if the viewer is lying on their back looking up into infinite celestial glory, warm golds and deep blues and pure whites, epic biblical illustration style, painterly realism, sense of infinite scale and divine power --ar 16:9 --v 6.1 --style raw

narration.content: Lehi read the book, and the Spirit of the Lord filled him. What he read was terrible and wonderful at the same time. He saw that Jerusalem would indeed be destroyed. Many people would die. Many would be carried away as captives to {{place:babylon:Babylon}}. But woven through the darkness was a thread of light — Lehi also read of a Messiah who would come, a Redeemer who would save the world.

When the vision ended, Lehi's heart was bursting. He praised God with everything he had: "Great and marvelous are thy works, O Lord God Almighty! Thy power and goodness and mercy are over all the earth. Because thou art merciful, thou wilt not let those who come to thee perish!" His whole soul rejoiced in what God had shown him.

But Lehi knew the vision was not just for him. He went out among the people of Jerusalem and began to tell them what he had seen — that the city would be destroyed, and that a {{person:jesus-christ:Messiah}} was coming. The people did not want to hear it. They mocked him. They grew angry. Just as they had done to prophets before him, they wanted to kill Lehi for daring to speak the truth. But God was watching over Lehi, and his story was only just beginning.

After Lehi had warned the people of Jerusalem and they had turned on him, the Lord came to him again — this time in a dream. The message was urgent and clear: "You must take your family and leave. Now. They are coming for your life." It was not a suggestion. It was a command.

And Lehi obeyed. Think about what that meant for a moment. He had a home, probably a nice one. He had gold and silver, precious things he had worked his whole life to earn. He had land that had been in his family for generations. And the Lord told him to walk away from all of it. Every last thing. Lehi packed up his family, loaded a few tents and enough food for the journey, and left it all behind.

narration.alt: Lehi's family departing Jerusalem into the wilderness with tents and provisions
prompts: An ancient Hebrew family departing through the gates of Jerusalem at dawn, a father leading the way with determination, a mother and four sons of varying ages following with loaded donkeys carrying tents and provisions, the massive stone walls of Jerusalem behind them glowing in warm morning light, the vast empty wilderness stretching ahead under a pale sky, a sense of both loss and courage, epic biblical illustration style, painterly realism, warm dramatic lighting, rich color palette, Arnold Friberg meets contemporary concept art --ar 16:9 --v 6.1 --style raw

A small family caravan seen from above, moving along a dusty trail away from a walled ancient city at sunrise, the city shrinking behind them as the rugged Judean wilderness opens ahead, long shadows stretching before them, warm amber light on the travelers contrasting with the cool blue shadows of the desert landscape, a mood of brave departure into the unknown, epic biblical illustration style, painterly realism, cinematic composition --ar 16:9 --v 6.1 --style raw

narration.content: They traveled south along the borders of the {{place:red-sea:Red Sea}} — Lehi, his wife {{person:sariah:Sariah}}, and their four sons: {{person:laman:Laman}} and {{person:lemuel:Lemuel}}, the two eldest, then {{person:sam:Sam}}, and finally {{person:nephi-son-of-lehi:Nephi}}, the youngest. After three days of walking through dry, rugged wilderness, they came to a valley with a river running through it that emptied into the Red Sea. Lehi set up camp and built an altar of rough stones. He knelt beside it and offered a sacrifice to God, thanking Him for bringing them safely this far.

Then Lehi looked at the river and had an idea. He turned to his oldest son, Laman, and said, "Oh, that you could be like this river — always flowing toward the fountain of all righteousness!" And to Lemuel he said, "Oh, that you could be like this valley — firm, steadfast, and immovable in keeping the commandments of the Lord!"

Why would a father say things like that to his own sons? Because Laman and Lemuel were not happy. Not happy at all. They grumbled and complained constantly. Their father was a "visionary man," they said — a dreamer who had dragged them away from their comfortable life in Jerusalem because of the "foolish imaginations of his heart." They didn't believe Jerusalem would really be destroyed. They thought the whole journey was a waste. And they were not shy about saying so.

narration.alt: Lehi's camp in the valley by the river, with tents pitched near water

A peaceful valley oasis in the desert wilderness at golden hour, a small river winding through green vegetation toward distant blue water, three simple tents arranged near the riverbank, a rough stone altar with thin smoke curling upward, rugged canyon walls rising on either side catching the warm light, a sense of temporary refuge in a vast wilderness, epic biblical illustration style, painterly realism, warm dramatic lighting, rich color palette, sense of scale and solitude --ar 16:9 --v 6.1 --style raw

narration.content: But Lehi was not a man who backed down. Filled with the Spirit of God, he spoke to Laman and Lemuel with such power that their bodies literally shook. They didn't dare argue with him after that. For the moment, they obeyed.

And then there was Nephi. Even though he was the youngest, there was something different about him. He was tall for his age and strong, but more importantly, he had a deep hunger to understand the things of God. When his father spoke of visions and commandments, Nephi didn't roll his eyes like his older brothers. Instead, he went somewhere quiet and prayed. He asked God to help him understand, and God answered him. The Lord softened Nephi's heart and filled him with belief. Unlike Laman and Lemuel, Nephi chose to trust his father.

Nephi told his brother Sam everything the Lord had shown him, and Sam believed. But Laman and Lemuel refused to listen. It broke Nephi's heart, and he prayed for them. The Lord spoke to Nephi and made him a promise: "Because of your faith, you will be blessed. I will lead you to a promised land — a land I have prepared that is choice above all other lands. And if your brothers rebel against you, they will be cut off from me. But you, Nephi, will be a ruler and a teacher over them."

It was a heavy promise for a young man to carry. A promised land somewhere out there in the unknown. Two brothers who might turn against him. And a father who now lived in a tent in the wilderness, trusting that God would show them the way. The journey was only beginning.
